key_insight: A physics-informed residual neural network can model hot deformation and dynamic recrystallization in a Mo-rich Ti alloy more accurately than conventional constitutive laws by embedding thermomechanical and DRX physics directly into the learning process.
engineering_relevance: This enables more reliable prediction of flow stress and recrystallization during forging and thermomechanical processing, supporting process design for advanced titanium alloys.
method: Stacked Residual Physics-Informed Neural Networks (STAR-PINNs) trained on flow-stress data from 800–1050 °C and 0.01–10 s⁻¹.
Enhanced model enforced constitutive constraints such as strain hardening, strain-rate sensitivity, thermal softening, and post-peak softening via automatic differentiation.
DRX-aware version added dual outputs, JMAK-Avrami regularization, DRX saturation constraints, and Arrhenius-consistency linking recrystallization fraction to stress.
evidence_signal: The DRX-Aware STAR-PINN achieved RMSE 11.69 MPa, MAE 4.83 MPa, R² 0.9850, and cross-validated RMSE 12.47 ± 0.26 MPa while reproducing flow curves, DRX kinetics, and Zener-Hollomon trends.
watch_item: Performance may depend on the quality and coverage of the experimental training data, so extrapolation beyond the tested temperature–strain-rate window should be treated cautiously.
paper_title: Physics-Informed Residual Deep Learning for Constitutive Modeling of Hot Deformation and Dynamic Recrystallization in a Mo-Rich $α+β$ Titanium Alloy
